channel.title: Namespace on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Namespace on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] Linux lxc Container Error — Fix
item.description:
Linux: lxc: container error

The lxc: container error message means the Linux Containers (LXC) runtime encountered a problem starting or managing a container. LXC provides system containers that run a full Linux OS. Errors can occur during container creation, startup, or networking setup.

What This Error Means

LXC uses kernel features (namespaces, cgroups, AppArmor/SELinux profiles) to create isolated system containers. When any of these subsystems fail — missing kernel support, configuration errors, AppArmor profile issues, or networking misconfiguration — LXC reports a container error and the container fails to start.

item.title: [Solution] Linux podman Namespace Error — Fix
item.description:
Linux: podman: namespace error

The podman: namespace error message means Podman encountered a problem with Linux namespaces used for container isolation. Namespaces provide process and resource isolation for containers. When namespace setup fails, containers cannot start. This commonly happens with rootless Podman when user namespaces are not properly configured.

What This Error Means

Podman uses Linux namespaces (PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC, USER) to isolate containers. In rootless mode, Podman creates a user namespace to map the container’s root to the host user. When the system doesn’t support user namespaces, subuid/subgid ranges aren’t configured, or storage is misconfigured, namespace operations fail.

item.title: [Solution] R Package or Namespace Load Failed
item.description:
What This Error Means

The error package or namespace load failed occurs when R cannot load a package even though it appears to be installed. This is often caused by missing dependencies, version conflicts, or corrupted installations.

Common Causes

  • Missing or outdated dependency packages
  • Version incompatibility between R and the package
  • Compiled code (C/C++) compilation failure
  • Corrupted package installation
  • Namespace conflicts with other packages

How to Fix

# WRONG: Just retrying library()
library(somepackage) # Error: package or namespace load failed

# CORRECT: Check dependencies first
pkg_info <- packageDescription("somepackage")
pkg_info$Depends
pkg_info$Imports

# Install missing dependencies
install.packages(c("dep1", "dep2"))

# WRONG: Ignoring version warnings
library(legacypackage) # Fails due to R version mismatch

# CORRECT: Check R and package versions
R.version.string
packageVersion("legacypackage")

# Update R or install compatible version
install.packages("legacypackage", type = "source")

# WRONG: Corrupted installation
library(brokenpackage) # Error after failed install

# CORRECT: Clean reinstall
remove.packages("brokenpackage")
install.packages("brokenpackage")

Examples

# Example 1: Diagnose load failure
tryCatch(
 library(somepackage),
 error = function(e) {
 cat("Error:", conditionMessage(e), "\n")
 cat("Suggested fix:", conditionMessage(e), "\n")
 }
)

# Example 2: Force reinstall from source
install.packages("package_name", type = "source", dependencies = TRUE)

# Example 3: Check loaded vs installed
search() # Shows currently loaded packages
installed.packages()[, c("Package", "Version")]

Related Errors

  • error-in-library — library() issues
  • error-in-install.packages — installation problems
  • error-in-source — sourcing R scripts

item.title: [Solution] Socket.IO Connection Error Fix
item.description:
Socket.IO Connection Error

This error occurs when Socket.IO fails to establish or maintain a WebSocket connection. It can involve connection failures, authentication issues, or namespace problems.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • Error: connect ECONNREFUSED (Socket.IO)
  • Socket.IO: connect_error
  • WebSocket connection to 'ws://...' failed

Socket.IO falls back to HTTP long-polling when WebSocket is unavailable. Connection errors indicate both transport methods failed.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Server not running
const socket = io('http://localhost:3000'); // ECONNREFUSED

// Cause 2: CORS misconfiguration
const socket = io('https://api.example.com'); // CORS error

// Cause 3: Authentication failure
const socket = io('http://localhost:3000', {
 auth: { token: 'invalid' },
});

// Cause 4: Namespace doesn't exist
const socket = io('http://localhost:3000/nonexistent');

How to Fix

Fix 1: Configure CORS on server

const io = new Server(httpServer, {
 cors: {
 origin: 'http://localhost:5173',
 methods: ['GET', 'POST'],
 },
});

Fix 2: Handle connection events

const socket = io('http://localhost:3000');

socket.on('connect', () => {
 console.log('Connected:', socket.id);
});

socket.on('connect_error', (err) => {
 console.error('Connection error:', err.message);
});

socket.on('disconnect', (reason) => {
 console.log('Disconnected:', reason);
});

Fix 3: Add reconnection logic

const socket = io('http://localhost:3000', {
 reconnection: true,
 reconnectionAttempts: 10,
 reconnectionDelay: 1000,
 reconnectionDelayMax: 5000,
});

Fix 4: Handle authentication

// Server
io.use((socket, next) => {
 const token = socket.handshake.auth.token;
 if (isValidToken(token)) {
 next();
 } else {
 next(new Error('authentication error'));
 }
});

// Client
const socket = io('http://localhost:3000', {
 auth: { token: getUserToken() },
});

Examples

// This triggers connection error
const socket = io('http://nonexistent-server:3000');

socket.on('connect_error', (err) => {
 console.error('Failed to connect:', err.message);
 // "Failed to connect: connect ECONNREFUSED"
});

Related Errors

  • ECONNREFUSED — connection refused
  • WebSocket Error — WebSocket error
  • CORS Error — CORS policy error

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2469 — X cannot be used as a namespace
item.description:
TS2469 — ‘X’ cannot be used as a namespace

TS2469 occurs when you try to use a module or class as a namespace. Only namespace declarations can be used with dot notation for sub-members.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using module as namespace
import React from "react";
React.createElement("div"); // OK if React has this export

// Cause 2: Trying to access sub-members of class
class MyClass {
 static value = 42;
}
MyClass.Nested; // TS2469: MyClass cannot be used as namespace

// Cause 3: Wrong namespace access
import * as utils from "./utils";
utils.sub.value; // TS2469 if 'sub' is not a namespace

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use proper import/require

import { something } from "./module";
// or
const { something } = require("./module");

Fix 2: Use static members correctly

MyClass.value; // access static property directly

Fix 3: Create a proper namespace

namespace Utils {
 export const sub = {
 value: 42,
 };
}
Utils.sub.value; // OK

Related Errors

  • TS2503: Cannot find namespace — namespace not found.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2503 — Cannot find namespace 'X'
item.description:
TS2503 — Cannot find namespace ‘X’

TS2503 occurs when you reference a namespace that TypeScript cannot find in the current scope. This can happen if the namespace is not declared, imported, or is in a different file without proper reference.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Referencing undeclared namespace
const val: MyNamespace.Type = {}; // TS2503: Cannot find namespace 'MyNamespace'

// Cause 2: Missing triple-slash reference
// file: types.d.ts declares namespace GlobalTypes
// file: app.ts uses GlobalTypes without reference

// Cause 3: Wrong namespace import
namespace A {
 export type Item = { id: number };
}
const item: B.Item = { id: 1 }; // TS2503

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add triple-slash reference

/// <reference path="./types.d.ts" />
const val: MyNamespace.Type = {};

Fix 2: Import the namespace

import { MyNamespace } from "./types";
const val: MyNamespace.Type = {};

Fix 3: Fix the namespace name

const item: A.Item = { id: 1 };

Fix 4: Use ambient declaration

declare namespace MyNamespace {
 type Type = { /* ... */ };
}

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.
  • TS2694: Namespace has no exported member — member not found.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2694 — Namespace 'X' has no exported member 'Y'
item.description:
TS2694 — Namespace ‘X’ has no exported member ‘Y’

TS2694 occurs when you access a member of a namespace that does not export that name. Only exported members are accessible from outside the namespace.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Accessing non-exported member
namespace MathUtils {
 const PI = 3.14; // not exported
 export function square(x: number) { return x * x; }
}
console.log(MathUtils.PI); // TS2694

// Cause 2: Typo in member name
namespace Colors {
 export const Red = "red";
}
console.log(Colors.Read); // TS2694: typo

// Cause 3: Wrong namespace
namespace A {
 export const value = 1;
}
namespace B {
 export const value = 2;
}
console.log(A.value); // OK
console.log(B.value); // OK

How to Fix

Fix 1: Export the member

namespace MathUtils {
 export const PI = 3.14; // add export
 export function square(x: number) { return x * x; }
}

Fix 2: Fix the member name

console.log(Colors.Red);

Fix 3: Use import in namespace

namespace MyNamespace {
 import Utils = OtherNamespace.Utils;
 export function use() { Utils.process(); }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2305: Module has no exported member — module variant.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.
  • TS2503: Cannot find namespace — namespace not found.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2749 — Refers to a Value But Used as a Type
item.description:
TS2749 — ‘X’ refers to a value, but is being used as a type here

TS2749 occurs when you use a value (like a class instance or variable) in a type position where TypeScript expects a type. This typically happens when importing a value instead of a type, or confusing class instances with class types.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using a class instance as a type annotation
class User {
 name: string = "";
}
const user = new User();
type MyType = typeof user; // This is the instance type

let x: user; // TS2749: 'user' refers to a value, but is being used as a type

// Cause 2: Importing a value instead of a type
import MyComponent from "./MyComponent";
type Props = MyComponent; // TS2749 if MyComponent is a value

// Cause 3: Using a variable name as a type
const config = { host: "localhost" };
type T = config; // TS2749

// Cause 4: Forgetting to use 'typeof' for value-to-type conversion
function process(input: string) {}
const fn = process;
type FnType = fn; // TS2749

Solutions

Fix 1: Use ’typeof’ to convert a value to its type

class User {
 name: string = "";
}
const user = new User();

// Wrong
let x: user; // TS2749

// Correct
let x: typeof user; // OK — x has the same shape as user

// Better — use the class name directly
let x: User; // OK — this is the instance type

Fix 2: Import types using ‘import type’

// Wrong — imports the value
import MyComponent from "./MyComponent";

// Correct — imports only the type
import type MyComponent from "./MyComponent";
type Props = MyComponent; // OK

Fix 3: Use the class name as the type

class User {
 name: string = "";
}

// Wrong — use the class name, not an instance
const user = new User();
function process(u: user) {} // TS2749

// Correct
function process(u: User) {} // OK

Fix 4: Extract the type from a variable using typeof

const config = { host: "localhost", port: 3000 };

// Wrong
type T = config;

// Correct
type T = typeof config; // { host: string; port: number }

Examples

// Example 1: Common mistake with module exports
// utils.ts
export const helper = () => {};
export type HelperType = { name: string };

// app.ts
import { helper, HelperType } from "./utils";
type T = helper; // TS2749 — helper is a value
type T2 = HelperType; // OK — HelperType is a type

// Example 2: Using enum as a type
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
type T = Color; // TS2749 — Color is a value (enum)
type T2 = typeof Color; // OK

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Cannot find name — identifier not in scope.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type issues.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module import issues.
